title: Americans Will Spend $485 Billion on Home Renovations in 2026 — And Most of It Is DIY
description: Home renovation spending is projected to hit $485B in 2026 as homeowners choose to improve rather than move in a high-rate market. DIY projects are leading the surge.
lede: With mortgage rates still above 6% and existing home inventory at historic lows, Americans are doing what they've always done when they can't move: they're renovating. The Joint Center for Housing Studies projects $485 billion in home improvement spending in 2026 — and an increasing share of that is going to DIY projects as homeowners turn to YouTube tutorials and online guides instead of contractors.

faqs.q: How much are Americans spending on home renovations in 2026?
faqs.a: The Joint Center for Housing Studies projects approximately $485 billion in home improvement and repair spending in 2026, up from $457 billion in 2025. This includes both professional contractor work and DIY projects.

Three factors: contractor costs have increased 30%+ since 2020, YouTube and online guides have made complex projects accessible to amateurs, and homeowners locked into low mortgage rates are choosing to improve rather than move.

faqs.q: What are the most popular DIY home projects?
faqs.a: The most common DIY projects include painting, flooring installation, shelving and storage, bathroom updates, landscaping, and hanging items on walls (TVs, shelves, art). Projects requiring electrical or plumbing work are still mostly done by professionals.

faqs.q: Is DIY renovation cheaper than hiring a contractor?
faqs.a: For simple projects (painting, shelving, minor repairs), DIY saves 50-80% versus contractor costs. For complex work (electrical, plumbing, structural), DIY can actually cost more if mistakes require professional correction. The sweet spot is cosmetic and mounting projects where the skills are learnable.

sections.heading: The Lock-In Effect
paragraphs: Nearly 60% of American homeowners have mortgage rates below 4%. Moving means giving up that rate and taking on a 6.5%+ mortgage — effectively doubling their monthly payment for a comparable home. The math makes renovation the obvious choice: spend $15,000 upgrading your kitchen instead of spending $150,000 more (in interest) to buy a different house with a nicer kitchen.

This 'lock-in effect' has created a renovation boom that hardware stores and home improvement retailers are riding. Home Depot and Lowe's both reported strong DIY-segment growth in recent earnings, with the fastest-growing categories being tools, mounting hardware, and paint.

sections.heading: YouTube Is the New Contractor
paragraphs: The biggest shift in home renovation isn't a new tool or material — it's information access. A decade ago, hanging a TV on a wall meant hiring a handyman for $200-400. Today, a 10-minute YouTube video shows exactly how to find studs, drill pilot holes, and mount the bracket. The skill premium has collapsed for dozens of common home projects.

The tools have gotten simpler too. Electronic stud finders now cost under $25 and are nearly foolproof. Laser levels sell for $15. Impact drivers have replaced the need for pre-drilling in most materials. The barrier to entry for basic home improvement has never been lower.

If you're starting a DIY project, our Stud Finder Guide covers five methods for finding studs in any wall type — with an interactive spacing calculator and comparison of the best stud finder tools on the market.

sections.heading: Where the Money Goes
paragraphs: Of the $485 billion in projected spending, roughly 35% goes to kitchen and bathroom remodels (still the highest-ROI projects for resale value), 20% to exterior work (roofing, siding, landscaping), 15% to systems replacements (HVAC, plumbing, electrical), and 30% to cosmetic improvements and repairs — the category where DIY dominates.

The fastest-growing segment is 'smart home integration' — homeowners adding smart thermostats, security cameras, automated lighting, and EV charging stations. These projects sit in a sweet spot: technical enough to feel like an upgrade, simple enough for a motivated DIYer with a YouTube tutorial.
